coredata.dc:description: Various neurodegenerative diseases (parkinson, huntington, alzheimer, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) are becoming serious global health challenges. Despite various treatment options, successful delivery and effective outcomes have been challenged with several physiological-anatomical barriers, formulation related issues, post-administration hurdles, regulatory constraints, physical hurdles, environmental issues, and safety concern. In the present review, we addressed a brief understanding of pathological and normal condition of blood brain barrier (BBB), rational for brain delivery using nanocarriers, major challenges, advantages of nanomedicine, critical aspects of nanomedicine to translate from bed to clinics, and strategic approaches for improved delivery across BBB. The review addressed various mechanistic perspective for delivery of drug loaded nanocarriers across BBB. Moreover, several reports have been published wherein phytomedicine, exosomes, magnetic nanopartilces, functionalized nanocarriers, cationic nanopartilces, and nano-phytomedicine were investigated for remarkable improvement in neurological disorders. These findings are informative for healthcare professionals, researchers, and scientists working in the domains. The successful application and convincing outcomes of nanomedicines were envisaged with clinical trials conducted on various drugs intended to control neurological disorders (NDs). Conclusively, the review addressed comprehensive findings on various aspects of drug loaded nanocarrier delivery across BBB, considerable risks, potential therapeutic benefits, clinical trial based outcomes, and recent advances followed by future perspectives.

Challenges and safety considerations of nanomedicine for brain delivery.

list-item.para: Morphology, functionalization, and surface charge of NPs may be altered after binding of protein to the surface.

The first issue to transport drug across BBB is to estimate or block the possible alterations. Current in-vitro studies revealed that size and surface charge density are crucial factors in immobilized protein to play important role.
Successful nanotechnology-based therapy depends upon the drug encapsulated, retention time in the target cells for maximum release.
Invasiveness.In brain fine blood capillaries, NPs are diffused on a surface area
(100 cm2 /g brain tissue). Thus, the NPs-therapeutics must be delivered to the targeted area.Even after crossing the thick and compact endothelial cell layer
(20–30 nm) of BBB, NPs are diluted again.

In the brain, NPs based systems face other issues against very tight barrier and high cell density per unit area. Purposely, NPs are designed to deliver drug to the target site at predetermined rate and expected concentration.Drug loaded NPs need to cross 3 different barriers (specialized endothelium of BBB or blood liquor barrier, glial cells and pericytes controlling the function of BBB)

The nature of material also significantly impacts on transport across BBB. Sometimes high binding of blood protein can also alter drug targeting and biodistribution.Nanocarrier-cell interactions sometimes cause accidental toxic events (ion channels blockage, altered membrane potential by nanoporation, and interfered signaling pathway)

Another hurdle is that cells inflict their own problems to a direct membrane crossing in the form of a protective glycocalyx. The cell membranes can differ in fluidity, surface charge, membrane asymmetry which slows down the permeability and leads NPs toxicity.
Positive NPs and polymers alter the membrane potential, and membrane integrity. These alterations are significant for regulating signalings in the CNS and a factor for neurotoxicity by NPs.
The neuron-based network in the brain is very complicated and complex which are composed of various cells for multiple functionalities.
The main risk of charged NPs interaction dependent upon synaptic chemical clefts.
Another probable chance of interrupted signalling pathways by NPs may result in ionic strength and pH to the cleft by complexing counter ions. The pH alteration may cause epilepsy or diabetic coma.

row.entry: (Sohaebuddin et al., 2010; Somjen , 2002; Zhao et al., 2009)

entry.bold: Challenges and safety considerations of nanocarriers

row.entry: The wide application of nanotechnology in the field of clinical neuroscience are progressing rapidly. Although several benefits of nanotechnology in the domains of neurobiology, neuro-pathophysiology, and molecular biology have been explored for disease diagnosis and therapy despite innate challenges.
Early detection of brain cancer, neurological abnormality, and CNS disorders in foetus are crucial challenges to control NDs using nanocarrier based systems.

To control multidimensional brain diseases, another significant limitation for nanomedicine is the need for multi-targeting molecule that may bind with multiple-receptors and play definite cellular and physiological responses.

Additional limitations for nanomedicines are the anatomy of BBB, cellular heterogeneity, and multi-dimensional cellular interlink in the brain.
The morphological features of nanoparticles sometimes alter the PK parameters, and bio-distribution of therapeutics that leads to systemic toxicity.

The environmental endangerment of nanocarrier is an additional safety factor due to their size, probable non-biodegradable nature, organic solvent retention, and exposure to atmosphere
Slow and sustained accumulation in animal, aquatic flora and fauna, and human to cause chronic toxicity and drug resistance

entry.bold: Regulatory challenges of nanocarriers based products

row.entry: Nanomedicines have widely considered in the drug delivery system while nanotherapeutics have been approved several years ago could have competition by follow-on versions if restricted regulatory rules existed. As these nanodrugs having some limitations like complex features, difficulties to evaluate, robust and consistent manufacturing strategies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Advantages of several nanocarriers based formulations for treating various NDs.

row.entry: Customised size, targeted functionalization, bioconjugation and slowed degradation. High drug loading.Polymeric NPs are capable to load lipophilic drugs whereas the hydrophilic shell can inhibit reticulate-endothelia system (RES) based uptake.
(Yu et al., 2020; Mu et al., 2013; Poustforoosh et al., 2022)
Controlled size, ease in surface functionalization and modification, hydrophilic polymer with high stability and possessing innate anti-aggregation properties
Solid Lipid Nanocarriers (SLN), NLCs, Nanoemulsion/Liposomes/ micelles systems/ Exosomes

Improved stability, ease in manufacturing, ease to scale up, biocompatibility, and biodegradable. NLCs (nanostructured lipid carriers) have improved drug loading over SLN. Increased drug access to CNS, reduced oxidative damage, and high protective activity on neurons.
Vesicle’s systems encapsulate both lipophilic and hydrophilic candidates with high drug entrapment, extended drug release, enhanced cellular uptake, high biocompatibility, and wide clinical uses.Several endogenous markers, adhesive protein, and specific vector ligands
(tetraspanins, integrins, CD11b, and CD18 receptors) are present on exosomes.
(Wang et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2020; Ananbeh et al., 2021; Haney et al., 2015; Lazar et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2021)

Metal NPs (gold, iron oxide, silver), carbon nanotubes, graphene dots, mesoporous
silica NPs

Inert, non-immunogenic, shapes variation, ease in surface-modification, size-based electrochemistry with Au, good permeation, and theranostic use. These can improve cognition, reduced oxidative stress, and related inflammatory responses. Gold NPs offer a high surface area and drug loading. Carbon dots have the capability to recognize single or small receptor binding site for probed ligands, and quantum in living cells employed for a long period. Au-NPs can be altered by surface alteration (large surface area, small size, high reactivity, and pore volume). Mesoporous silica NPs provide a controlled drug release profile and high encapsulation efficiency.
(Chen et al., 2016a,b; Maiti et al., 2019; Palomaki et al., 2011)

head.ce:title: Strategic nanocarriers to control neurodegenerative disorders: Concept, challenges, and future perspective

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various neurodegenerative diseases (parkinson, huntington, alzheimer, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) are becoming serious global health challenges. Despite various treatment options, successful delivery and effective outcomes have been challenged with several physiological-anatomical barriers, formulation related issues, post-administration hurdles, regulatory constraints, physical hurdles, environmental issues, and safety concern. In the present review, we addressed a brief understanding of pathological and normal condition of blood brain barrier (BBB), rational for brain delivery using nanocarriers, major challenges, advantages of nanomedicine, critical aspects of nanomedicine to translate from bed to clinics, and strategic approaches for improved delivery across BBB. The review addressed various mechanistic perspective for delivery of drug loaded nanocarriers across BBB. Moreover, several reports have been published wherein phytomedicine, exosomes, magnetic nanopartilces, functionalized nanocarriers, cationic nanopartilces, and nano-phytomedicine were investigated for remarkable improvement in neurological disorders. These findings are informative for healthcare professionals, researchers, and scientists working in the domains. The successful application and convincing outcomes of nanomedicines were envisaged with clinical trials conducted on various drugs intended to control neurological disorders (NDs). Conclusively, the review addressed comprehensive findings on various aspects of drug loaded nanocarrier delivery across BBB, considerable risks, potential therapeutic benefits, clinical trial based outcomes, and recent advances followed by future perspectives.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Some critical neurodegenerative disorders [Alzheimer disease (AD), Parkinson disease (PD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Huntington disease (HD)], affect a major population of human globally. AD is the most common and accounts for 60–70 % of all neurodegenerative cases. Patho-physiologically, AD is associated with extracellular accumulation of Aβ-peptide and tau protein deposition within neuron (Baker et al., 2018). PD is caused due to gradual damage of dopamine releasing neurons within the substantia nigra and pathological aggregation of the fundamentally uncombed protein α-synuclein into Lewy bodies (Braak et al., 2003). The third “ALS” causes molecular loss in motor neurons; therefore,understanding of the pathogenesis and motoneuron degeneration is crucial (Stämpfli et al., 2019). The fourth “HD” (a gene-related neurodegenerative disorder) is caused by the increase of the trinucleotide repeat sequence (CAG) (Kay et al., 2018). There are very limited treatment options for these brain disorders. Some significant components like peptides and genomic agents generally do not reach the desired site due to the presence of BBB (blood brain barrier), extensive first-pass metabolism, and toxic outcomes on non-target peripheral sites (Meredith et al., 2015). The parenchyma of brain is made up of polarized endothelial cells attached with the help of tight junction of the cerebral endothelium of capillaries, and several transporters that cause very low permeation and transportation of therapeutic drugs intended for CNS delivery (Rehman et al., 2017). Although, the structural features of BBB are altered in the brain disorders and still remained as a major manifestation (Daneman et al., 2012). Current research studies are developing such components (<100 nm in size) with desired features (Boado et al., 1995). Nanocarriers are playing a promising role rather than other drug delivery approaches intended to deliver across the BBB. Nanoparticles are preferable due to their size and functional features, to overcome these issues. Several nano-sized delivery systems, including liposomes, stealth liposomes (Xiang et al., 2012) microspheres, nanoparticles, nanogels, and dendrimers, have been utilized to enhance the bioavailability of therapeutics in the targeted brain site. Among them, polymeric nanoparticulate systems are reported to be more efficient in treating brain diseases with certain limitations for bio-actives delivery. Nanomedicines have gained much attentions by the researchers, scientists, and health professionals to control neurological disorders. For the enhancement of pharmacokinetic properties of poor soluble therapeutics by improved solubility in lipophilic matrix of nanoparticles. Moreover, these nanocarriers offer targeted drug delivery through innate passive transport for reduced toxicity and side effects. Thus, nanotechnology-based formulations are able to improve safety, efficacy, pharmacokinetic, and bioavailability of drug suchas such as PEGylated liposomes (Rip et al., 2014). Commonly used nanocarriers for biomedical use are vesicular systems (micelles, liposomes, elastic liposomes, ethosomes, and niosomes), polymeric and lipid based NPs (dendrimers and solid lipid nanoparticles) (Klementieva et al., 2011), nano capsules (nano-shells), inorganic NPs (metallic NPs), nucleic acid-based NPs, microemulsion (Chaiyana et al., 2013; Jogani et al., 2008), silica nanodepletor (Jung et al., 2020), and magnetic NPs, which enhance the therapeutic window of active chemotherapeutic drugs compared to the delivery through conventional approaches by showing steady-state therapeutic levels of therapeutics over a prolonged period of time (Kumari et al., 2016; Amin et al., 2017; Bondì et al., 2010; Debnath et al., 2017; Frozza et al., 2011; Jaruszewski et al., 2014; Kakkar et al., 2013, Alam et al., 2018; Alam et al., 2020; Alam et al., 2021). Nano formulation-based therapy increases the solubility of lower soluble bio-actives and decreases the metabolism by dissolving into the hydrophobic or hydrophilic compartment, and also the formulations reach the specific site. Liposomes functionalized with acidic lipid rescues AB-induced toxicity as explored in murine neuroblastoma cells (Bereczki et al., 2011). Additionally, nanocarriers can internalize with physiological membrane for passive absorption and subsequently target specific tissue or organs for maximized drug access to the site of action. However, there is a need for tissue targeting via nanocarriers by applying advanced active targeting approaches, ligand probed carriers, surface functionalization, and prodrug synthesis (Biswas et al., 2014). Several neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) result in enhanced degeneration of the structural and functional response of the CNS. The siRNA-based therapy is considered one of the most potent therapeutic approaches to manage NDs (Mishra et al., 2022).stealth liposomes (Xiang et al., 2012)

Strategically, data were systematically collected from various search engine database (PubMed, google scholar, google, Springer nature, web of science, scopus, and publon), publishing journals of Elsevier, future medicine, Wiley, MDPI, ACS, and others. We used “keywords” in search tab, and further filtration (filter types vary by database) using year, author, disease, and related words as per need of running heads (or subheads). Relevant findings were searched by combining terms (OR and AND) in specific way to broaden or narrow the findings. Moreover, search was unbiased by inclusion and exclusion criteria in database search tab. We could not include findings of unrelated topics.

Patients with positive family history of neurodegeneration are at a higher risk of neurodegenerative disorders. However, exact reason is still unknown. The major risk factor for initiating neurodegeneration involves increasing age (>65 years) being sensitive to neurodegeneration (Tanner et al., 1992). Malformations linked to memory and motor functions are common among older people. In Europe, ∼ 35 % of diseases are linked with NDs, and ˃ 1.5 billion populations are affected by neurological conditions. As of 2021, almost 6.2 million Americans are suffering from AD. There are almost ten important disease states which influence the brain tissue and central nervous system (CNS) (neurodegenerative, neuro-inflammatory, and neoplastic disorders) (Blasi et al., 2007). AD, PD, ALS, and HD (Huntington disease) are the most common under the NDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blood-brain barrier (BBB): Normal and pathological BBB
ce:section.ce:para: The BBB is made up of a complex tight surface of capillary endothelial cells having several enzymes, efflux pumps, receptors, and responsible to restrict certain molecules to enter into the CNS. In Fig. 1 , a pathological BBB is quite different from normal physiological architecture in terms of anatomy, vascularization, and cellular composition. The main hurdle for managing NDs is the transportation of bio-actives through the BBB. The vesicular transport mechanism is mainly significant for RMT (receptor-mediated transport) through the BBB, as shown for molecular shuttles which increase antibody delivery to the targeted site within brain. Due to the physiology of the BBB, the ability of compounds to passively cross the BBB via diffusion is extremely limited. Proteins, apolipoprotein (Apo-E mediated) (Kreuter et al., 2010), and peptides are required for brain homeostasis cross the BBB via transcellular transport. The NPs passively diffuse via BBB being small nanosized (<400 Da). Carrier-mediated transport mechanisms are majorly involved in the transport of a maximum number of drug-loaded NPs to the brain because of nano size and surface functionalization. Through the RMT mechanism, endogenous molecules can transport easily into the brain effectively with low efficacy than endothelial cells of the peripheral systems. Binding of therapeutics to their specific receptors present on the luminal side of endothelial cells, activates the endocytosis process. Additionally, another transport mechanism, adsorption-mediated transcytosis (AMT) helps the therapeutics to cross BBB. Polymeric NPs such as liposomes, poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) and poly (lactic acid) (PLA) NPs, PBCA, and inorganic composites like zinc oxide, gold, and silver nanoparticles have all been developed and used to penetrate through the BBB (Tu et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2017; Loureiro et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2020) as shown in Fig. 2 . Table 1 summarized various challenges for delivery of drug loaded nanocarriers across BBB to control NDs.Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The rationale for the employment of nanomedicine to control NDs
ce:section.ce:para: NPs are classically divided by their structural composition. Generally, the nano formulation-based drug delivery systems are convincing option to control AD and PD due to having unique features (ease to cross tight cell junctions, across the BBB, improved bioavailability, drug protection, and high drug encapsulation efficiency) and specificity against mutagenic proteins present in brain disorders (Hagl et al., 2015). Polymeric NPs and multifunctional liposomes can decrease the aggregation of β-amyloid plaques and functionalized liposomes enhances the cellular drug uptake as a multipurpose nanocarrier to cross the BBB (Barbara et al., 2017; Papadia et al., 2017). Micelles can inhibit the Aβ-induced oxidative stress and TNF (Calzoni et al., 2019). The BBB surface is rich with several receptors which can definitely bind with respective ligands and boosts the internalization into cells. Therefore, the specific ligands can be modified onto NPs to conduct the transport via BBB. The definite characteristics of nanomaterials like decreased size, biocompatibility, longer retention time in systemic circulation, and non-toxicity, have been utilized for targeted delivery. The nanotechnology-based drug transport systems are based on both specific and non-specific strategies to target the specific brain sites (Sun et al., 2017). Inactive therapeutic molecules such as antibodies, antibody fragments, and peptides bind to bio-actives and transporting systems to function as homing tool at the target site such as rationally designed mitochondrial targeted drug loaded polymeric nanoparticles to control cancer, AD, and obesity (Marrache and Dhar, 2012). Direct bio-active injection and catheterization are examples of passive drug targeting and also known as physical targeting which is dependent upon development of a bio-active carrier conjugate. Continuous bio-active aggregation in leaky vasculature area is a type of passive targeting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Critical aspects of nanomedicine to translate for clinics
ce:section.ce:para: For the advancement of nanotherapeutics to the clinic, two significant parameters must be considered, firstly the toxicity or safety profiles of ‘novel nanotherapeutics’ and the secondly the recognition of scale-up methods required for industrial purposes (Kesharwani et al., 2018). Therefore, to use nano bio-actives to treat neurodegenerative diseases, the development of such system is required where experienced physicians, neurobiologists, expert on nanotechnologists, pharmacologists, and toxicologists are involved actively. Basically, the scientific organization observes the defragmentation of the knowledge into scientific, clinical, and industrial fields. The major function is to investigate these isolated aspects as actors to begin the architecture of skill differing from general knowledge of pathophysiology and of the BBB. In brief, a general aspects of critical factors has been illustrated in Fig. 3 . These approaches are (a) approach I. Neurobiology of disease and nanotherapeutics (nanodrugs characteristics, various aspects of the anatomy and pathophysiology of NDs, BBB in healthy and diseased brains), (b) Neurotherapeutics of nanomedicine (specific ligands for targeting), (c) Translatability of nano-neurobioactives (bench-to-bedside steps, and reliability of animal models), (d) Neurotoxicology of nanomedicine (safety of nanotherapeutics) and regulations for nanotoxicology, (e) Nanomedicine scale-up (scale-up of nanocarriers, toxic effects, suitability of laboratory protocols in nanocarriers formulation).

This is a physical-based method wherein the proteins and nutrients possessing low bioavailability are administered through intravenous or oral route to cross BBB and subsequently improved bioavailability (Pautler et al., 2010). These methods involve intracerebroventricular infusion, convection-elevated transport, and intracerebral injections or brain implant. In few cases, BBB is mechanically infringed by giving osmotic shock, MRI-guided breached BBB, and by applying bradykinin analogue to give an open transportation through brain (Gabathuler et al., 2010). Partridge et al. reviewed a detailed information on various CNS barriers (arachnoids and blood-cerebrospinal fluid barriers, BBB structure, and BBB-brain endothelial cells) to drug delivery, drug design and modification for facilitated transcellular transport, barrier bypass delivery methods (intranasal, intrathecal, interstitial, interstitial bolus injection, and implant), and biophysical methods (focused ultrasound BBB disruption) (Partridge et al., 2022). Recently, Teleanu et al. updated current advancement on various strategic approaches for enhanced drug delivery across BBB and tabulated pros and cons of invasive methods. Authors reviewed that BBB transient disruption and intracerebrovascular methods are beneficial for transient opening to achieve high drug concentration and high gene transaction efficiency, respectively (Teleanu et al., 2022). BBB transient disruption and intracerebrovascular strategies are associated with limited clinical use due to whole BBB disruption and safety concern, respectively. Kobo-Greenhut et al. studied a comparative study between non-invasive nose to brain drug delivery and invasive brain delivery. This was a patient centered care impact analysis involving five stages such as (1) demand identification, (2) ranking demands impact magnitude, (3) scoring demand compliance, (4) demand priority assignment based on impact magnitude and compliance, and (5) generating a summative impact priority number (Kobo-Greenhut et al., 2022; Sonvico et al., 2018; Sood et al., 2013).

In this approach, the drug loaded nanocarriers or free drug is directly delivered through parenteral administration into cerebral ventricles (lateral) which subsequently diffuses to the brain through cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). Notably, the diffusion of such drug administration is limited due to innate barriers (parenchyma) and other factors. However, the administered drug reaches to the targeted site only close to the ventricles in the brain. This may be attributed to complex anatomy of BBB, permeability related factors of choroid plexus, and brain parenchymal cells (Dasgupta et al., 2016). Monoclonal antibodies based drug delivery is challenging due to high molecular weight and insolubility in aqueous system. Barzegar-Fallah et al. reported a detailed review compilation on harnessing ultrasound technique for targeting brain and breaching BBTB (blood brain tumour barrier) (Barzegar-Fallah, et al., 2022). Various methods have been adopted in literature to bypass BBB/BBTB to improve drug delivery locally to tumor tissue of brain such as convention-enhanced delivery, direct intracranial injection, and controlled rug release from polymer matrix but with the cost of high risk of mortality, morbidity, and invasive open surgery. Authors reviewed several clinical trials reports and data were tabulated for ultrasound based drug delivery to target brain tumour (NCT02253212, NCT02343991, NCT03712293, NCT03626896, NCT03616860, NCT03714243, NCT03744026, NCT03551249, NCT04021420, NCT04528680, NCT04614493, NCT04440358, NCT04417088, NCT04804709, and NCT04063514) and Alzheimer diseases (NCT02986932, NCT03119961, and NCT03671889) (Barzegar-Fallah et al., 2022).

This is a stereotactic based delivery strategy where there is direct delivery of drugs to the targeted sites of brain using stereotactic apparatus. Such apparatus is the catheter(s) introduced via cranial burr hole into brain and therapeutic drug is actively pumped into brain parenchyma (Spector et al., 2015). This method offers numerous advantages over other invasive techniques like transportation of high-molecular-weight active compounds, better distribution of the therapeutics, and cause no cerebral edema in intracranial pressure (Cunningham et al., 2008). CED is an innovative and promising localized drug delivery to control brain related disorders such as cancer and neurodegenerative disorders. To understand drug efficacy and transport, in silico methodology can be used for CED treatment simulation and prediction of drug deposition in CED regimes using clinical imaged data. Therefore, Lambride et al. compiled various aspects (parametric simulation and prediction such as size of drug, blood vessels pore size, tumour tissue hydraulic conductivity, different histopathological conditions) of in silico methodology for simulation and prediction based on CED (Lambride et al., 2022). Moreover, to deliver drug into brain tumor, ultrasound assisted CED was used and evaluated by numerical and experimental evidences. Authors investigated the impact of focused ultrasound on drug delivery to brain tumour using artificial brain mimicking gel (mimicking brain microenvironment) followed by injection and focused ultrasound. The test sample was injected into the gel and FUC (resonance frequency of 1.1 MHz) was applied at the same site of injection (gel) followed by evaluating pre-existing equations of literature using developed finite model element. Thus, ultrasound assisted CED achieved 500 % higher drug volume distribution as compared to CED alone (Boroumand et al., 2022). Notably, MRI guided CED was also applied for visualization of degradation in brain tissue (Prezelski, et al., 2021).

Pharmacological approach is well established and widely used to control NDs. In this, high molecular weight compound (protein and peptides), and hydrophilic drug are modulated for improved bioavailability to the targeted sites in brain. The method involves chemical processes and colloidal therapeutic carrier methods.

Chemical methods involve the alteration of the chemical structure of the bio-active which improves numerous physicochemical characteristics like lipophilicity and enhances the therapeutic efficacy (Bidros et al., 2009). By the addition of lipid moiety to the polar ends of drug molecules leads to improved BBB crossing efficiency. The prodrug approach is another kind of chemical modifications where therapeutics are altered in such a way that, they must convert into the active form by metabolic processes after systemic administration (Lu et al., 2014). There are many molecules reported to have prominent potential to transiently open BBB for high drug access to brain after systemic administration as standalone or co-administered with active drug. These act either by transient BBB disruption or decreased TJ protein expression (claudin-1, occludin, and tricellulin). Cereport (a bradykinin analogue), regadenonson (a adenosine receptor agonist), borneol, and puerarin were known for improved BBB enhancer and these execute remarkable therapeutic efficacy by increased drug transport across BBB in animal model. However, these were failed to execute the same efficacy in human when used as alone or in co-administration (Dong, 2018). Various probes were explored for guided delivery across BBB and these controlled AD and Parkinson diseases in disease induced animal model. These probes are transferrin, angiopep-2, lactoferrin, insulin like growth factor, and lectin. Surface functionalization of nanocarrier using probe offers maximized internalization with BBB for enhanced and targeted drug delivery to control AD and PD. To culminate the accumulated Aβ protein of diseased brain, surface functionalized NPs with an iminodiacetic acid inhibited Aβ aggregation and assembly (super-large mesoporous silica NPs) as evidenced with an in-vitro model and AD model mice [Liu et al., 2017; Jung et al., 2020]. Similarly, a peptide 4F conjugated angiopep-2 lipidic NPs reduced the p-Tau level and remarkably improved the learning and memory ability in AD model mice [Han et al., 2022]. In PD, polymeric NPs conjugated with probe or surface functionalized exhibited efficient and effective outcome in animal model. For this, PLGA NPs loaded with levodopa and puerarin laoded PLGA NPs improved motor behaviour in PD animal model (Liu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2019). Methylene blue (MB) is involved to interupt tau-mediated multiple pathways of AD pathogenesis. Therefore, hyperphosphorylated tau-targeted multifunctional nanocomposites could be a promising approach to control neurological disorder (Chen et al., 2018).

In this method, microscopically dispersed therapeutics are tailored as nanosuspension (colloidal suspension) for parenteral delivery. Commonly utilized colloidal systems nano emulsion, NPs, micelles, liposomal products, nanocrystals and polymeric nanocarriers (dendrimers) for effective and efficient delivery across BBB through several transcellular processes (endocytosis and transcytosis) (Aso et al., 2019; Klajnert et al., 2006; Klajnert et al., 2012; Sharma et al., 2017). These have significantly improved PK parameters, and biodistribution to control brain disorders (Kreuter, 2001). Various findings have been explained in section 8. Several authors reviewed various nanomaterials used to control PD (Ghazy et al., 2020; Gilmore et al., 2008). Joshi and Thakur reported nanoparticles to control HD (Joshi and Thakur, 2014). Lactoferrin coated liposomes augmented amyloid deposition mediated toxicity (Kuo and Wang, 2014).

This method uses physiological and transporter mediated drug delivery across BBB. The technique is based on the fact that certain ligand molecules (insulin, glucose, lectin, transferrin, lactoferrin, angiopep-2, low density lipoprotein as LDL, and growth hormone) are actively transported through surface transporters to the certain sites of brain (targeted drug delivery) [Chen et al., 2019; Dong, 2018; Jung et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2020]. There are some define transporters which help to deliver specific molecules such as transferrin and insulin receptors. This method involves receptor mediated, peptide mediated and adsorptive mediated transportation across cells.

For peptide mediated transportation of active drug, commonly basic amino acid carrying positive charge is preferred to improve the transportation of active molecules through BBB (Batrakova et al., 2008). The reaction between cell penetrating peptides and cell surface is receptor independent, which causes drug delivery into the cytoplasm and the nucleus of brain cells (Dissanayake et al., 2017). The transport into the cell involves two major mechanisms, energy-independent direct penetration across the plasma membrane and energy-based endocytosis (Guidotti et al., 2017). This method has been widely investigated in pre-clinically and also evaluated clinically in cancer patients. AmyP53 is a new class of molecule to target a molecular mechanism common to AD and PD, with high therapeutic potential (Fantini, et al., 2020; Yahi et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Receptor existing in BBB and receptor mediated drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: Fundamentally, several surface receptors of endothelial cells of BBB (insulin, transferrin, and low-density lipoprotein receptors) are overexpressed and these are targeted by the definite ligand, modulated, and finally reaches into the target site after successful crossing of BBB (Warso et al., 2013). It was found that the antibody against transferrin receptor and insulin receptor effectively transport the bioactive through receptor-mediated transcytosis (Jones et al., 2007). Additionally, the diphtheria toxin receptor and LDL receptor related protein also assist to transport the therapeutics into the brain. Fig. 4 portrayed various receptor mediated drug delivery across BBB.

The majority of proteins and macromolecules, including growth factors, immunoglobulins, and other compounds, cannot penetrate the BBB on their own. However, using the relevant transporters, some of these proteins can pass through the BBB. A few receptors, such as the transferrin receptor (TfR), insulin receptor (IR), lipoprotein receptors, and rage (receptor for advanced glycation end-products) (LRP1 and LRP2) (Ito et al., 2017; Deane et al., 2004; Urich et al., 2012) (Fig. 2) mediate the transports through the BBB. To date, therapeutic antibodies and enzymes have been delivered to the CNS with great frequency using TfR and IR (Pardridge et al., 2012). The Alzheimer's A toxin is cleared from the brain and into the blood thanks to LRP1 (Bell et al., 2007). An advanced method for delivering drugs to the brain that has great specificity, selectivity, and affinity is transcytosis, which is mediated by the receptor. However, because of competition between natural ligands and medications, the ligand may affect homeostasis and the targeting efficiency may decline (Wei et al., 2014). The high expression of TfR on ECs makes it one of the most popular receptor for targeted delivery to the brain. OX26, a mouse monoclonal antibody against rat TfR, can also use this receptor to cross the BBB in addition to transferrin. The most crucial route for the biomacromolecules to cross the BBB is receptor-mediated transcytosis. This is so because the majority of these bio-macromolecules cannot pass the BBB by themselves. The RMT ligand recognition system is extremely sophisticated. Therefore, this mechanism could only carry specific chemicals between the blood and brain (Zhao et al., 2015). Biomacromolecules go through the BBB at a slower rate on average than nutrients. The receptors for transferrin, insulin, and leptin are transferrin receptor (Bray et al., 2015), insulin receptor (Ito et al., 2017), and leptin receptor (Zlokovic et al., 2011), respectively. Additionally, lipoproteins are transported across the BBB by means of lipoprotein receptors found on its surface. The RMT system could be used by the antibodies against the aforementioned receptors to provide transportation through the BBB.

In this method, transcytosis and endocytosis of a compound are quite different in comparison with receptor mediated transcytosis and endocytosis. In a nonspecific strategy of endocytosis, adsorptive endocytosis releases the peptides and proteins from the luminal plasma membrane of the cell. Several peptide vectors have been designed and evaluated in adsorptive-mediated drug transport into targeted brain tumours (Demeule et al., 2008). Therapeutically active candidates are unable to cross BBB in their free form and limited clinical significance. Therefore, this limitation was bypassed by surface functionalized apolipoprotein E of polymeric nanoparticles (Mulik et al., 2010; Hartl et al., 2020; Kreuter, 2001; Bell et al., 2007; Re et al., 2011). Coating with tween 80 or poloxamer 188 further facilitated surface adsorption of Apo-E onto NPs surface (Hartl et al., 2020).

Mainly-two major categories of nanocarriers are used for NDs (organic and inorganic nanocarriers) as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 5 illustrated various in vitro, ex vivo and in vivo models (Teleanu et al., 2019). Scientists have developed various NCs which defend the siRNA from fast enzymatic decomposition, decrease immunogenicity, enhance blood retention time, and offer targeted siRNA delivery.

Cationic phosphorus dendrimer was investigated for brain delivery to control AD (Wasiak et al., 2015). Among vesicular systems, cationic lipoplex (a similar structure to cell membranes) internalises by electrostatic interactions with brain tissue or by functionalization and surface modification for targeted brain delivery (Dimov et al., 2017; Byeon et al., 2016; Rotman et al., 2015). Additionally, multifunctional liposomes and dual functional nanoparticles (Zheng et al., 2017; Balducci et al., 2014) are also used to manage AD, to target sequentially, and also in treating glioma to realize dual targeting response and precision distribution in the brain (Kuo et al., 2017). Exosomes can be used as vehicles to treat several neurodegenerative diseases successfully. The research studies concluded that exosome proteins get aggregated in the plaques of AD patient brains where β-cleavage of amyloid precursor protein generates and can traffic around the body of amyloid precursor protein in brain ( Rajendran et al., 2006; Sharples et al., 2008). Glycosphingolipid-enriched exosome infusion results in a reduction in Aβ levels and ameliorates Aβ related pathology in APP (amyloid precursor protein) mice (Yuyama et al., 2014).

Polymeric nano micelles by PEGylated polymeric nanomicelle which results in decreased AB-amyloid aggregation and subsequent neurotoxicity (Pai et al., 2006; Scialabba et al., 2012; Sikorska et al., 2014) offer various advantages and have resulted in the effective drug delivery. Conjugation or adsorption technique can be applied in the core or on the surface of nanocarriers such as gold, iron, and silica based NPs for brain delivery (Wang et al., 2017; Xiong et al., 2017). Among the nanoparticles, only gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have reported low cytotoxicity, predominant optical features, proved synthesis process, and having promising ability to cross the BBB by changing the size. It is reported that gold nanoparticles are able to stop cognitive deficits, oxidative stress, and inflammation in the AD rat model (Nguyen et al., 2000). Polyamidoamine (PAMAM), polypropylene imine (PPI), and polylysine dendrimers are very much favourable for both hydrophobic and hydrophilic bio-actives. The large molecular weight entities combine with dendrimers (Cy5–d-mino) and easily overcome the BBB than the free therapeutics. Two reasons to approve while utilizing PAMAM dendrimers for neuroscience purposes are safety profile (limiting surface modification) and reproducible preparation manner (Florendo et al., 2018). Do et al. used magnetic NPs (electromagnetic actuator) carrying therapeutic drug to cross BBB in presence the of external magnetic field [28 mT (0.43 T/m) or 79.8 mT (1.39 T/m)] for significantly improved cellular uptake and drug transportation rate of MNPs (magnetic NPs) (Do et al., 2016).

Nanogels offered many advantages like ease to cross BBB, reduced toxicity, improved cellular uptake, high encapsulation, drug targeting, capable to encapsulate both hydrophilic and hydrophobic therapeutics, and drug release at a predetermined rate to the desired site following ionizing radiation-engineered based technique (Liu et al., 2015; Picone et al., 2018; Picone et al., 2016), cubosomes based drug access (piperine) (Xiao et al., 2016) and oligonucleotide delivery into BBB (in vitro model) (Vinogradov et al., 2004). The in-vivo studies revealed that nanogel enhanced the brain uptake of oligonucleotides by reducing its uptake in the liver and spleen. The surface modulation of nanogel with transferrin and insulin increases the distribution of drugs via BBB (Picone et al., 2016; Picone et al., 2018).

A significant improvement in nanoscience is the synthesis and application of QDs (semiconductor nanocrystals). Tang et al., 2018 reported graphene quantum dots (GQDs) able to reduce the aggregation of Aβ1-42 peptides (Tang et al., 2018). Kim et al., 2018 utilized APP/PS1 transgenic mice, where they applied a complex of GQDs and a neuroprotective peptide holding glycine-proline-glutamate (GQDG). GQDs can protect dopamine neurons against matured fibers (Kim et al., 2018). Having a small size of GQDs, able to cross the BBB, the reaction between α-syn and GQDs is beneficial for PD’s ethicology (Frank et al., 2014; Guerrero et al., 2021). QD-labelled Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF) (QD-BDNF) was developed by coupling mBtBDNF with QD655 that may be applied to identify the mechanisms of disrupting the axonal mechanism in HD (Lin et al., 2016).

Carbon nanotube, onion, and dots (CDs) are promising approach at present scenario due to unique features (enhanced solubility in physiological solvents, high surface area, rapidly modulated with bio actives, and biocompatible with neuronal systems) as brain-specific treatment strategy by surface modification (Ma et al., 2018). Zhang et al., has described the electrically conductive property and strong mechanical characteristics of CNTs, and revealed the morphological features of CNTs that are identical to degenerative neurons (Zhang et al., 2009). Matsumoto et al., revealed that MWNTs coated with nerve growth factor influenced the progression of neurons on a CNT scaffold, and also controlled the development and pursued the viability of the neurons (Matsumoto et al., 2007). CDs are featured with physical and optical properties (like feasible fluorescence, stability, aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, cost-effective). Encapsulation of neuroprotective peptide (glycine proline-glutamate) into the core of GQDs inhibited the aggregation and decreased various pro-inflammatory cytokines (like IL-1, IL-6, IL-33, IL-17a, and tumour necrosis factor-TNF) (Xiao et al., 2016). The human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) is responsible for the initiation of brain toxicity where CDs were efficiently used as a promising option to disaggregate hIAPP (Wang et al., 2018). As CDs are capable of crossing the BBB, the modified CDs has successfully applied to block the aggregation of Aβ that may be utilized for AD management (Chung et al., 2017). CDs were modulated (by hydrothermal process) by applying ammonium citrate as a carbon source and further decorated with branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI) (Malishev et al., 2018). As a result, the developed CDs were able to block BACE1 that help to inhibit fibrillation and toxicity of Aβ (Han et al., 2017). It was found that the expression of Aβ and APP can be decreased by applying CDs as neurological components, for AD management (Zhou et al., 2019). Above mentioned reported cases indicate that modified CDs can be utilized for various NDs by using promising nanocarriers, which can easily penetrate the BBB. Carbon onion (zero-dimensional nanostructure) shows various unique advantages such as wide absorption band, greater surface area to volume ratio, biocompatibility, and thermal stability (Plonska‐Brzezinska et al., 2019). Another research reported that carbon onions were designed to deliver an anti-Alzheimer’s bio-active that penetrated the BBB. These water-soluble CNOs (wsCNOs), in the size range of 25–50 nm, were developed and entrapped an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (Pakhira et al., 2016).

There are several non-viral vectors available like Retroviral vectors (RV), Lentiviral vector (LV), herpes simplex virus (HSV), adenovirus (AV), and adeno-associated virus (AAV). These have shown efficient drug transport efficiency into the brain. RV is not used in neurodegenerative disorders but few research studies are currently claiming fibroblasts loaded RV can inhibit the degeneration of cholinergic neurons and enhances the cognitive responses (Conner et al., 2001). A study revealed that RV encapsulated NGF gene is under phase 1 clinical trial for cognition improvement and as AD management therapy (Tuszynski et al., 2005). Further study revealed that the transportation of CD34 + hematopoietic cells manipulated applying LV for adrenoleukodystrophy and metachromatic leukodystrophy management in children (Biffi et al., 2013). Neprilysin (NEP) is an Aβ decomposing enzyme which was reported for AD mitigating through inhibiting beta-amyloid plaques. Spencer and his co-workers involved neprilysin in LV by utilizing CMV as a promoter and transported it to APP-Tg mice and result found that Aβ aggregation decreased by 48 % in the cortex region and 25 % fall measured in the hippocampus part (Spencer et al., 2008). Peng et al., used LV with siRNA which could able to knockdown the BACE1 gene, hampered the β-secretase generation, and reduced Aβ plaques aggregation (Peng et al., 2010). LV also contaminates the astrocytes because of its cellular tropism against astrocytes and therefore LV is considered as an effective choice for AD management (An et al., 2016).

Sonoporation (intravenously injected microbubbles, MBs) shows more targeted specific drug delivery by sonopermeation for improved permeability (BBB) of drug loaded carrier system. Pore development does not need high-energy ultrasound and causes no injury to the tertiary tissues, and thus increases the chances of transportation of nanocarrier through brain parenchyma (Fisher et al., 2019). Ultrasound complex with viral microbubbles holding BDNF leads to increase in transfection efficacy of brain neurons, initiates the high expression of exogenous gene BDNF, and plays crucial therapeutic role in AD model rats (Wang et al., 2021).

This technique involves the introduction of nucleic acid-based therapeutic agents including plasmid DNA, siRNA (Cooper et al., 2014; Van den Boorn et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2020), and microRNA to control AD, PD, and HD (Alvarez-Erviti et al., 2011; Didiot et al., 2016). In PD, gene therapy has been considered for initiating the exposition of neuroprotective genes, such as glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF) or to down-regulate the expression of α-synuclein (Darquet et al., 1997; Niu et al., 2017; Axelsen and Woldbye, 2018; An et al., 2010). Gold and magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles both are being utilized to arrange the plasmid DNA impeding SNCA (gene as a locus in PD) to neurons with the aid of complexing Nerve Growth Factor (NGF) that improves neuronal encapsulation (Amin et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2018). By applying magnetic iron oxide, nanoparticles were also used as a pH-dependent and thermosensitive polymer (N-isopropylacrylamide) to consider intracellular transfer rather than the extracellular release of DNA (Kojima et al., 2018). Exosomes have solved the immunoreactivity problems of conventional viral vectors in terms of gene treatment. Gene delivery using lactoferiin-modified nanoparticles has been explored to brain targeting to control Parkinson in rodent model (Huang et al., 2010). In a study, authors formulated nanoparticle (dendrigaft poly-l-lysin) loaded with RNA responsible for silencing caspase-3 gene and subsequent inhibition of activation of the same enzyme. For this a virus peptide (29 amino acids) was linked to the nanoparticles for crossing BBB through specific receptor mediated transcytosis mechanism. The image analysis tool revealed that nanoparticles were preferably accumulated in brain than nontargeted organ after intravenous administration as shown in Fig. 9 (Liu et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phyto nanocarriers as a phytomedicine to control NDs
ce:section.ce:para: NDs mainly linked to old aged patient and are prevailing fast globally because of enhanced elderly population and one of major limitation of to treat NDs is lack of effective therapy. Currently the researchers have focused on the progression of integrative medicine for the management of various NDs. Green synthesized medicines are better choice over synthetic drugs as they show various adverse effects, that makes limited options for treatment. To avoid the serious toxic effects of synthetic therapeutics, scientists are focusing on the development and the use of phyto-nanomedicines which have several advantages like antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-amyloid characteristics (Niu et al., 2017).

These theranostic have not been used successfully for the management of NDs like AD and PD whereas the synthetic molecules show some limitations like toxicity and high production cost. Conventionally, the green synthesis method is followed the medicinal plants or phytochemicals which show pharmacological actions and offer chelation and stability to the NPs (Lopresti et al., 2017). Currently, Suganthy and co-workers reported green gold NPs to load Terminalia arjuna and showed protective neuroprotection and better biocompatibility by blocking AChE (acetylcholinesterase). Benzothiazolinone was fused with curcumin and showed greater binding affinity towards amyloid-beta which can be a promising targeted approach of curcumin or several other natural products for the management of AD (Roy et al., 2017). Streptozotocin (SZT)-treated male albino mice were given thymoquinone (TQ) TQ-encapsulated PLGA NPs fabricated with polysorbate 80 that imitates AD oxidative stress by reducing Streptozotocin (SOD) activity (P-80-TQN). ). The application of thymoquinone in depression was evaluated by preparing the lipid vesicles using solvent injection method (Alam et al., 2018). The pharmacodynamics activity result showed significant enhancement in the tested parameters in rats with satisfactory results and in vitro –in vivo correlation needed to check the similarity. The same research group prepared thymoquinone loaded solid lipid nanoparticles and evaluated its therapeutic efficacy in depression on animal model (Alam et al., 2020). The animal group treated with thymoquinone SLN depicted greater therapeutic efficacy than the pure thymoquinone. In subsequent year, thymoquinone loaded solid lipid nanoparticles showed better therapeutic efficacy against depression (Alam et al., 2021).

Several clinical trials of nanocarriers ferrying drug intended to control NDs have been conducted and reported in literatures. Some of them have been summarized in Table 3 . These are gold nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, nanocrystals, and gold nanocrystals. Researchers and healthcare professional and scientists explored for diagnosis and therapeutic application of these nanomedicine for efficacious delivery and therapeutic outcomes. In general, CNS diseases such as multiple sclerosis, Parkinson disease and other related neurological disorders were targeted for better clinical expected outcomes.

Although nanocarriers show greater penetration of the BBB, there are several manifestations that must be overcome. First, a greater number of nanocarriers are being absorbed into the brain, and afterward, this accumulation can result in potential risk to the particular organ. Second, some inorganic nanomaterials (gold nanoparticles, iron nanoparticles, cerium oxide, and silica nanoparticles) do not get metabolized easily, resulting in aggregation in the brain (DeCoteau et al., 2016; Migliore et al., 2015). They help in neurodegeneration by influencing mitochondrial impairment, redox variance and damage, cytoskeleton shattering, neuroinflammation, and microglia activation (Migliore et al., 2015). The magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles resulted in neuronal disappearance in the chicken embryo and cerium oxide nanoparticles can impede the discrimination of neural stem cells (Patel et al., 2019; DeCoteau et al., 2016). It was also reported that biodegradable nanoparticles also cause neurotoxicities, such as after introducing polysorbate 80-modified chitosan nanoparticles resulting in cell death, damage of neurons, and few inflammatory actions in the frontal cortex (Yuan et al., 2015). Furthermore, chitosan nanoparticles (200 nm) resulted in deformities such as the bent spine, pericardial edema, and opaque yolk in zebrafish embryos (Oberdörster, 2004). Most of the nano-formulations are injections, although the oral dosage forms are mainly used in neurodegenerative diseases. Therefore, further studies (including acute toxicity, long-term neurotoxicity) in this portion must be conducted. Finally, environmental consideration is now gaining attentions and EPA (environmental protection agency) capped by strict guidelines due to nanomedicine mediated global challenges. Nanomedicine based surface coating on the floor, wall, and window curtain in hospitals results in multiple drug resistance due to regular washing and frequent sweeping. The released antibiotics into drainage and wastewater cause aquatic lives in danger and ultimately human health. These must be controlled to save our ecosystem from toxic ecosystem. Drugs and nanomaterials reaching soil, water, and air (air borne particle based lungs disease) are causing several health issues. Thus, there must be a proper assessment of the risk-benefit ratio in the development of nanotechnology based drug delivery to brain (Migliore et al., 2015).

Despite several advancement in nanotechnology based drug delivery to control CNS neurodegenerative disorders, there are still lack of solutions to resolve the upcoming and persisting challenges. These are clinical, pharmaceutical, and environmental related issues. Biogenic routes of drug development using plant extract could be a promising approach to control neurological disorder [Suganthy et al., 2018]. At industrial scale, tech transfer from laboratory to clinical bed or large scale productions are still challenging for nanomedicine based formulation/product success. These are related to physical and chemical instability, desired size and size distribution particularly in parenteral product. Lots of variable parameters become critical to control at large scale production. Process variables become completely different and challenging during tech transfer before execution of the optimized batch. Therefore, various nanomedicines are still not commercialized for controlling CNS disorders.

Pharmaceutical challenges are related to formulations wherein composition, types and content of excipients, interaction and compatibility, instability, and product cost are prime considerations.. Nanomedicines derived from synthetic, semisynthetic, and natural excipients sound good for patients and regulatory bodies as well (Ovais et al., 2018). However, limited natural and biocompatible excipients are available to translate a poor soluble or poor permeable drug with high expectation after oral/topical/parenteral delivery. Incompatibility, limited storage stability due to particulate aggregation or Oswald ripening, drug leakage over time and storage condition, regulatory stipulated terms, low chemical stability, and high cost burden are certain relevant considerations for developing a successful nanomedicine. This can be exemplified with an example of artesunate. This is a salt of succinic acid used to control cerebral malaria. The drug is highly unstable in water based product. Therefore, there is not a single aqueous based parenteral injection in market. US-FDA could not approved any injection so far. In the market, a kit is available with water for injection, sodium bicarbonate and lyophilized artesunate. After reconstitution (as per label), clear solution must be injected within 20 min. Otherwise, it starts to precipitate due to instability in water. Thus, transforming artesunate or similar types of drug become a challenging task for a formulation scientist particularly for CNS delivery.

At clinical level, several issues are still unresolved for nanomedicine. These are the factors responsible to interact with nanocarrier within in vivo condition. In vitro study is used to predict and simulate in vivo performance. However, shape, size, and performance of nanomedicine are uncontrolled and unpredicted over course of time in vivo. There are limited or no model available to assess real time changes in morphology inside body. Considering safety concern, components must be selected as per toxicity profile well documented in animal model and human. Polymeric nanomedicines are still challenging due to toxic metabolites generated after in vivo exposure. Maximum daily exposure dose further limits the use of excipients for desired dose nanocarrier based drug delivery across BBB. Still, additional investigation is needed for the better advancement of nanocarrier systems from a pharmacokinetic and toxicological view for success in clinical trials. Magnetic, inorganic, and functionalized nanomedicines have a wide area of interest with several existing as well as promising technical utilization and scientific issues undefined. Dendrimer surface functionalization is still limitation for safety concern. The advancement in the field of molecular, cellular biology and in modern biomedicine enabled a close understanding of the CNS intrinsic barriers, that indicate the innate safety of brain structures against various exogenous and endogenous molecules such as antigenic and therapeutics. The intrinsic properties basically led by size-dependent and surface-related characteristics. Certain treatment strategy is not approved for chronic neurodegenerative diseases, the collaboration between healthcare researchers, materials scientists, and engineers give valuable and promising approaches towards the modern therapy desideratum.

Emerging science of nanomedicine with neuroscience is a game of risk and benefit. Nanomedicines are well explored for brain delivery but transport across BBB and safety concerns are yet to be determined. These nanocarriers may cause neurotoxicity (excitotoxicity, DNA damage, neuroinflammation, and allergic responses) due to toxic components responsible for oxidative stress, BBB disruption, and amino acid disturbance. Nanoscale size results in particles aggregation and poor drug loading (Vega-Villa, et al., 2008). There is no particular method to determine the toxic level and site specific drug release in the CNS. Thus, the current nanotechnology based clinical utility needs to be improved further so that these can be used safely and targeted so far.

Overall, nanocarriers are showing a promising role in neurodegenerative disease treatment by supporting and assisting the functional regeneration of impaired neurons, giving neuroprotection, and enhancing the delivery of neuroactive drugs, genes, and cells across the BBB. Nanocarriers in presence of specific ligands (such as glucose, lactoferrin, transferrin, and specific peptides) cross the BBB and transfer the bio-actives to targeted sites. Although this technique is still at the primary stage, some specific limitations (like brain distribution, stability, and loading efficiency) must be overcome before the clinical stage of neurodegenerative purposes. Moreover, we should focus on targeting the lesion site, not only the brain. Furthermore advancement of nanocarriers can provide a significant role in drug delivery systems to treat various NDs. Various new options, influenced by current improvement in nanotechnology, and earlier used for therapy purposes must be re-evalauted for improved therpaeutic benefits. For providing various safe and effective advantages over conventional approaches in the case of brain disease, NPs can be used for efficient brain drug delivery to the targeted site. Based on their application, the major factors are surface chemistry, hydrophobicity, shape, and size, and charge. After all, the NPs must have the same mandatory properties such as being biocompatible, decreased toxicity than another existing system, must not degrade easily, controlling the release of drugs to the targeted site, easily capable of binding and transporting active bio-actives. All the mentioned features are needed for the efficient penetration of NPs via BBB. To improve the BBB crossing efficiency the surface of NPs is modified with appropriate ligands, which leads to the proper distribution of drugs to the affected brain site. The urgent need for therapeutic approaches to CNS disorders and the limitations caused by existing systems recommend the advancement of nanotechnology-based targeted drug delivery. Therefore, depending upon the findings and research reports it is concluded that nanotechnology-based drug delivery for several NDs would be great option for neurosurgeons, neurologists to offer scientific processes in collaboration with pharmaceutical scientists for developing technological progression in clinical neuroscience.
Authors would like to extend their appreciation to the Deanship of scientific research at King Khalid University supporting this research through small group program under Grant number RGP.1/58/43.

Sumel Ashique: Conceptualization, Software. Obaid Afzal: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Sabina Yasmin: Funding acquisition. Afzal Hussain: Validation, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Mohammad A. Altamimi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Thomas J Webster: Methodology, Formal analysis. Abdulmalik S.A. Altamimi: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Validation.
